panelReasoning: Unanimous 4/4 for D. An AND gate outputs 1 only when every input is 1, so '1 AND 1 is 1' is the unique true statement; A and C both assert the FALSE claim '1 AND 1 is 0' and B asserts the false claim '1 AND 0 is 1' (the OR/NAND confusion trap). The source-archaeologist rendered page 21 of both copies of 'KOC 2012 EXAM' (different md5) and found the A/C duplication is a real typo in the printed paper, faithfully carried into the digital item, with 'D. 1 AND 1 is 1' bold+underlined in both. The defect therefore lands on a wrong option and does not touch key uniqueness: exactly one true option remains on the ballot. No extraction error; item is defective in printing but sound in key and gradable as printed.

finalAnswer: Q(t+1) = D regardless of the present state: Q=0,D=0 -> Q(t+1)=0; Q=0,D=1 -> Q(t+1)=1; Q=1,D=0 -> Q(t+1)=0; Q=1,D=1 -> Q(t+1)=1
panelReasoning: Unanimous 4/4. A D flip-flop latches its input on the active clock edge, so its characteristic equation is Q(t+1) = D with the present state Q absent: the four rows are (0,0)->0, (0,1)->1, (1,0)->0, (1,1)->1. Two judges executed the state equation and got exactly those rows; three judges read /Users/meshal/Developer/sandbox/knpc_reviser/public/figures/knpc2016-q007.png and confirmed a Q | D | Q(t+1) table with rows 000/011/100/111. The source-archaeologist rendered page 29 of 'KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf' at 400dpi: item 7 is printed as an open short-answer question with the completed table supplied by the paper itself, and the shipped figure is a pixel-faithful crop of it. Extraction is clean and the stored answerText matches the paper. Key confirmed. Non-blocking presentational note (does not affect the key, so no flag and no review badge): the completed table is currently attached as stemFigure, so the card displays its own solution; per SPEC it belongs in answerFigure, revealed only after the answer.

Unanimous 4/4 for C (Dijkstra). The source-archaeologist rendered page 29 of 'KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf' and read the printed item ('5) Shortest path is solved by which algorithm? a. Bellman-Ford b. Prim c. Dijkstra d. DFS') with 'c. Dijkstra' bold+underlined under the file's inline answer-key convention, corroborated by neighbouring items 6/8/9 on the same page. Extraction matches the paper exactly. Rejections: B (Prim) is definitionally a minimum-spanning-tree algorithm - the intended trap, and its presence plus the paired item 4 ('minimum spanning tree is solved by' -> Prim) proves the author's contrast axis is the standard Dijkstra/Prim pairing; D (DFS) finds a path, not the shortest (executed: DFS returned [0,3,1,6,9] vs the true [0,3,1,4,7]). Standing caveat carried in the explanation, not a dispute: A (Bellman-Ford) is a genuine single-source shortest-path algorithm (executed: identical distance vector to Dijkstra) and is strictly more general, so the item is technically flawed as single-select - but every judge, including the one who read the paper, keys C. If a future paper offers Bellman-Ford WITHOUT Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford is the pick.

3-of-4 for A alone; the skeptic dissents with [A, D] (not an unanswerability claim), so under the panel rule this ships badged as key_disputed rather than resolved. The content is not in doubt: FDDI = Fiber Distributed Data Interface (ANSI X3T9.5 / X3.139-1987, ISO 9314). B 'Fiber Data Distributed Interface' is a word-swap trap; C 'Fiber Dual Distributed Interface' is the designed trap for anyone who studied the dual counter-rotating ring topology instead of the acronym - the second D is Data, not Dual. The source-archaeologist rendered page 7 of '[CE] questions.pdf' and confirmed the A==D duplication is REAL in the printed paper (the Q62 option block is even typeset in a different font, suggesting a paste error), then rendered page 2 of '[CE] solutions.pdf' and read the Model Answer table row 62 -> 'A. Fiber Distributed Data Interface', verifying row alignment against neighbours 58/59/61/63. So: no extraction error, the official key is A, but as printed two labels carry the same correct text and the item cannot be graded fairly on label. Ship with the amber badge; a student who marks D has the right text and must not be counted wrong. Repair path is to replace option D with a genuine distractor - never to change the key.

QUARANTINE. The skeptic rules it unanswerable and two other judges sit below the 0.6 confidence bar (distractor-analysis 0.15, skeptic 0.55-band), which triggers the quarantine rule outright. On substance three judges converge: the stem 'safest way to connect to a secure network through a public network' is the textbook definition of a VPN / encrypted tunnel (IPsec, WireGuard, SSL-TLS), and NEITHER printed option is a security mechanism - TCP (RFC 793) gives ordered reliable delivery with zero confidentiality, integrity or authentication, and UDP (RFC 768) gives less. The 'TCP is least-wrong because TLS rides on TCP' rationalisation does not survive the counter-reading: OpenVPN, IKE/IPsec, WireGuard and DTLS all run over UDP, so the least-wrong argument points at both options depending on which repair you assume - the signature of a broken item. IMPORTANT correction to the other judges' premise: the source-archaeologist rendered page 30 of 'KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf' and showed the two-option list is NOT a pipeline truncation - the paper itself prints only 'a) TCP b) UDP' with 'a) TCP' bold+underlined. The extraction is faithful; the SOURCE is broken (this KNPC 2016 section is a recalled paper - option 'd) I don't remember' appears verbatim elsewhere). So the item is un-repairable without the real option set and must not be served: shipping it would teach 'TCP = secure', which is false. Study fact to carry instead: safest connection to a private network across a public network = VPN / encrypted tunnel.

QUARANTINE. The skeptic rules it unanswerable (0.55) and two other judges are below the 0.6 bar (first-principles 0.5 for 'Transport', distractor-analysis 0.2), triggering the quarantine rule. The source-archaeologist rendered page 31 of 'KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf' and confirmed the extraction is faithful: the paper prints '18) Datagram is which layer?' with a single bold+underlined line 'a) Transport' (item 17 is missing entirely; 15 and 16 are likewise stubs), so the existing no_distractors flag and inline_bold provenance were both correct and the pipeline lost nothing - the RECALLED SOURCE is what is mutilated. That is exactly why the item cannot be keyed: the deciding evidence (was 'Network' on the ballot?) is gone, and both readings are textbook-backed. Bare 'datagram' = the NETWORK-layer PDU in Kurose & Ross, Forouzan and RFC 791 ('internet datagram' = IP); 'user datagram' = the TRANSPORT-layer PDU of UDP (RFC 768) and the OSI PDU tables of the era pair Datagram(UDP) with layer 4 and Packet with layer 3. A single bolded option in a from-memory recall is not strong enough to overturn the dominant textbook convention, and it is equally consistent with mis-remembering. Do not serve as a keyed card. Convert to a two-sided recall fact: IP-flavoured stem -> Network; UDP-flavoured stem -> Transport.
